input_text:
Voyager 1 embarked on its journey from Cape Canaveral on September 5, 1977. This launch occurred sixteen days after its counterpart, Voyager 2, a sequence that seemed reversed due to the distinct flight paths NASA assigned to the two probes. Utilizing a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket, the primary objective of the mission was five years of observation, involving flybys of Saturn and Jupiter during a fortunate, infrequent configuration of the outer planets, an alignment occurring approximately every 176 years.

The probe arrived at Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this encounter, it transmitted the initial detailed visuals of the planet’s faint ring system and discovered active volcanoes on its moon Io—marking the first documented instance of such volcanism beyond Earth. Twenty months following this Jupiter pass, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 reached its closest point to Saturn. It documented the ring system with unmatched clarity and captured detailed images of Titan. This Saturn trajectory propelled Voyager 1 beyond the solar system's plane, concluding its planetary circuit but directing it into interstellar space.

On February 14, 1990, following the recommendation of astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 aimed its instruments back toward Earth, taking a picture when it was approximately 6 billion kilometers distant. This solitary image, appearing as one pixel against the sunlight, earned the title Pale Blue Dot. This represented the last photograph taken by the spacecraft, after which the cameras were powered down to maintain energy reserves.

Voyager 1 officially entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, crossing the heliopause boundary, making it the first human-made object to achieve this status. It carries the Golden Record, a 12-inch, gold-plated copper phonograph disc curated under Sagan’s leadership. This record contains greetings in 55 languages, alongside music and imagery intended for any civilization that might eventually locate it. Its power source relies on three radioisotope thermoelectric generators, fueled by plutonium-238, whose half-life of 87.7 years has steadily diminished the available energy.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is situated approximately 25 billion kilometers from Earth and is traveling at roughly 61,000 kilometers per hour. In November 2026, it will become the first probe to span a distance equivalent to one light-day.
output_text:
Voyager 1 began its mission from Cape Canaveral on September 5, 1977. This launch occurred sixteen days after Voyager 2, despite the seeming reversal caused by the unique flight paths allocated by NASA to the two probes. Both spacecraft were launched using a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket. The primary goal of the mission was to conduct five years of observation, which involved flybys of Jupiter and Saturn. This configuration of the outer planets, which occurs roughly every 176 years, was fortunate and rare.

The probe reached Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this encounter, Voyager 1 transmitted the first detailed visuals of Jupiter's faint ring system and uncovered active volcanoes on its moon Io, documenting volcanism beyond Earth for the first time. Twenty months later, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 performed its closest approach to Saturn. It documented Saturn’s ring system with unparalleled clarity and captured detailed images of Titan. This trajectory around Saturn guided Voyager 1 beyond the plane of the solar system, concluding its planetary tour but setting it on a course into interstellar space.

Following the suggestion of astronomer Carl Sagan, on February 14, 1990, Voyager 1 turned its instruments back toward Earth, taking one photograph when it was about 6 billion kilometers away. This single image, appearing as a solitary pixel against sunlight, earned the title Pale Blue Dot. This was the final picture taken by the spacecraft before the cameras were powered down to conserve energy.

Voyager 1 officially entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, upon crossing the boundary of the heliopause, thus becoming the first human-made object to achieve this status. It carries the Golden Record, a 12-inch, gold-plated copper phonograph disc curated under Sagan’s direction. This record includes greetings in 55 languages, along with music and visuals intended for any future civilization that might find it. Its power supply comprises three radioisotope thermoelectric generators, which run on plutonium-238; the half-life of 87.7 years of this isotope gradually diminishes the available power.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is situated approximately 25 billion kilometers from Earth and travels at about 61,000 kilometers per hour. In November 2026, it will become the first probe to traverse a distance equaling one light-day.
